strengths: Comprehensive, specific and measurable success criteria covering security, functionality, usability and performance
Thoroughly justified rationale for choosing PHP/MySQL, clearly aligned with the clientâs environment and needs
Clear, realistic scenario with direct client quotations to situate the investigation
weaknesses: Consultation evidence is limited to a single interview excerpt; broader requirements-gathering artifacts are missing
Rationale is cut off mid-sentence and lacks any comparison with alternative technologies
Scenario description, while clear, does not explore prioritization of features or multiple consultation iterations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning section shows strong success criteria (band 6) and a fully justified product rationale (band 5), but the scenario/consultation strand is only in the 3â4 band due to limited evidence of requirements-gathering.  Taken holistically, the work sits solidly in the 3â4 band overall, meriting 4/6.

strengths: Moderate complexity with dual-role access control, real-time availability checks, hashed passwords and email notifications
Effective, appropriate use of PHP, MySQL and XAMPP in line with intended purposes
Some source attribution and technique explanation present
weaknesses: No use of version control or automated-testing tools; advanced IDE features are not demonstrated
Documentation of borrowed code and libraries is incomplete, with no formal bibliography
No evidence of advanced algorithms or sophisticated data processing beyond CRUD and email integration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Complexity and tool use place the work in the 5â8 band, while documentation/source attribution is only in the 1â4 band.  Holistically, the development sits at the lower half of the 5â8 band, meriting 5/12.

strengths: Detailed flowcharts and UI mock-ups that clearly describe main user journeys and interface design
A complete, structured test plan mapping each success criterion to test types and example actions
weaknesses: No chronological record of tasks or development log is present
Flowcharts lack exception flows and data-structure/ER diagram details
Test plan omits explicit expected results and a testing schedule
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The solution overview is undermined by the complete absence of a task record (band 0), despite strong design overview (band 3â4) and test plan (band 5â6).  Overall performance aligns with the lower end of the 1â2 band, justifying 2/6.

strengths: Screenshots indicate that core features run without fatal errors
weaknesses: No video evidence or systematic demonstration of full product functionality
No examples or diagrams showing an extensible architecture or modular design
No sample extensions or plug-in mechanisms provided
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Functionality is only partially demonstrated (band 1â2) and no concrete extensibility mechanisms are shown (band 1â2).  The combined evidence supports the top of the 1â2 band, so 2/4.

strengths: Client quotations are mapped to success criteria, showing some client-centred evaluation
Realistic and feasible future-development recommendations are provided
weaknesses: No quantitative performance data or metrics in the evaluation
Feedback, while present, is not deeply analysed or prioritised
Recommendations are not prioritised by impact/effort and lack a timeline
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although there is an evaluation table and some feedback, the lack of quantitative metrics and prioritisation means the work remains within the limited attempt band (1â2).  Overall, 2/6 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: Multiple flow-charts describe all main processes for both user roles, and UI mock-ups convey layout, colour scheme and navigation.  The overview is clear and shows how components interact, demonstrating a sound understanding of development.  Some diagrams lack detailed data structures or hardware/software interaction, so the work is comprehensive but not of the exceptional depth expected for the 5-6 band.

The rationale explains in full why a PHP / MySQL stack was selected, linking the choice to the clientâs existing technology environment, ease of deployment, performance considerations and scalability.  Each point is explicitly justified rather than merely listed, meeting the descriptor for a fully justified rationale.  Minor omissions (e.g. alternative solutions not compared in depth) prevent the maximum mark, but the work clearly sits in the top band.

PHP, MySQL, and XAMPP are used in a way that matches their intended purpose.  The student discusses hashing, queries and email integration, showing appropriate and effective tool use.  Evidence of advanced IDE features, version control or automated testing is missing, so the strand warrants the lower half of the 5-8 band.

The test plan lists each success criterion, specifies test type, nature and example actions.  It addresses security, functionality, usability, integration and performance, providing a structured basis for systematic testing.  Expected results are implicit but not always spelled out, so while detailed and complete, it falls slightly short of perfectionâplacing it at the lower end of the top band.

The student proposes realistic enhancementsâadvanced filters, onboarding tutorial, analytics, payment integration, responsive designâand briefly justifies how each would improve the product.  The recommendations are feasible and well reasoned, aligning with the top band, though the justification is concise rather than exhaustive, so a 5 is appropriate instead of 6.

The submission references âAppendixâ quotations and acknowledges some sources (e.g. Canva for prototypes) but lacks a formal bibliography and detailed explanation of borrowed code or libraries.  Basic technique explanations are present, giving partial adherence to requirementsâplacing the work mid-band (1-4).
No chronological record of tasks (e.g. dated entries, iterations, problems encountered) is provided in the submission, so the strand must be awarded 0.

The product handles dual user roles, real-time availability, hashed passwords and email notificationsâfeatures that indicate a moderate level of complexity beyond basic CRUD.  Ingenuity is evident in integrating role-based access and automated emails, but no advanced algorithms or sophisticated data processing are documented.  This sits comfortably in the 5-8 band but nearer its lower end.

Thirteen success criteria are stated and each is specific, measurable and relevant to the clientâs needs (e.g. hashed passwords, role-based access, email notifications).  The list covers security, functionality, usability and performance, constituting a comprehensive range.  This aligns fully with the highest band.

Client feedback is provided in the form of multiple direct quotes linked to criteria, evidencing consultation.  The feedback is specific, moving the strand beyond âlimitedâ into âsome,â warranting the 3-4 band; the number of quotes but lack of broader discussion merits the lower mark of 3.

While the student states the system is âscalable,â no design patterns, modular structure diagrams or extension examples are supplied.  Expansion seems possible but implementation details suggest it could be difficult.  This aligns with the middle bandâs description; however, evidence is weak, so the lower mark in that band is appropriate.

The student describes a clear real-world scenario (difficulty booking soccer fields) and identifies a real client (Mr Melo).  Direct quotations from an interview are woven into the narrative, demonstrating that at least one consultation has taken place.  However, the consultation evidence is limited to a short interview extract and does not explore broader requirements-gathering (e.g. meeting minutes, multiple iterations, prioritisation).  The level of detail is therefore appropriate but not yet "thorough," fitting best in the 3-4 band.

The evaluation section and screenshots imply the site runs and features are operational, but no video or systematic evidence is included.  Functionality therefore can only be judged as âpartially demonstrated,â fitting the 1-2 band; awarding the top of that small range recognises the breadth of claimed features.

A table matches each success criterion with a pass/fail and supporting client quotation, giving more than a âlimited attemptâ but lacking depth such as quantitative results or explanation of unmet criteria.  This is a partial evaluation, suitable for the 3-4 band at the lower end.

annotations.comment: The scenario description is clear and situates the client effectively, demonstrating a realistic context for the solution.
Evidence of client consultation is present but limited to one interview excerpt. Document additional meetings or requirements-gathering artifacts to strengthen consultation evidence.
The rationale provides strong justification for the PHP/MySQL stack but omits comparison with alternative technologies. Include a brief alternatives analysis.
The rationale section starts a sentence abruptly and is cut off. Complete the explanation to ensure the rationale reads as a cohesive argument.
The feature overview is descriptive but does not prioritise or differentiate core versus nice-to-have features. Consider ranking by client impact.
The continuation of success criteria maintains clarity and scope, capturing security, functionality, and usability effectively.
Justifying server setup with XAMPP is clear, but the clientâs existing infrastructure constraints could be detailed to show deeper alignment.
The success criteria list is comprehensive and each item is specific and measurable, offering a clear basis for evaluation.
The login page flowchart is clear but lacks error and validation paths. Incorporate exception flows such as failed login attempts.
A chronological record of tasks is missing. Include dated entries or a development log to track progress and iterations.
Flowcharts cover main user journeys but lack underlying data structure or ER diagrams. Add database schema visuals to clarify interactions.
The design narrative is informative but does not specify hardware or network requirements. Outline system requirements to complete the overview.
The field owner login flowchart omits input validation and edge cases. Illustrate how invalid credentials or system errors are handled.
UI mock-ups demonstrate layout and color scheme but do not show responsive behavior. Provide mobile-view mock-ups or responsive breakpoints.
The prototype narrative explains design intent but could link to a live prototype or repository to facilitate review.
The test plan lacks a proposed schedule or timeline. Add dates and responsibilities to coordinate systematic testing phases.
The field reservation flowchart is well-structured but omits error handling for unavailable slots. Include decision nodes for overbooking prevention.
UI diagrams are helpful but lack annotated interaction sequences or user story mapping. Add labels to show user flow through each screen.
The test plan table is detailed and systematically maps each success criterion to test types and actions, supporting thorough testing.
Expected results are implied but not explicitly stated. Define clear pass/fail criteria for each test to guide verification.
The evaluation table maps all success criteria to client quotes, demonstrating thorough client-centred evaluation.
Evaluation lacks quantitative metrics (e.g. response times, error rates). Incorporate measurable performance data to deepen analysis.
The recommendations are realistic, feasible, and aligned with client priorities, showing good foresight for future development.
Recommendations could be prioritized by impact and effort. Include a simple prioritisation matrix to focus next steps.
Performance success criterion (âreal-time checksâ) should specify maximum acceptable latency to make evaluation measurable.
The scalability criterion mentions structured architecture but lacks detail on patterns or components. Elaborate on how scalability is achieved.
